purpose: Section-specific generate prompt for authoring a principle's solution field (approach + key_benefits). Composed at runtime with system_prompts/generate.json.
system_addendum:
SECTION YOU ARE AUTHORING: solution

The solution field tells the reader HOW to implement the principle. The statement field named the rule; the problem field named the failure mode the rule prevents; the solution field names the concrete artefact, where it lives, how it's wired into the lifecycle, and what enforcement catches violations. Gates and evidence both build on the solution — gates reference the artefacts solution names; evidence references the artefacts solution makes inspectable.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD SOLUTION

A principle's solution is { approach: string, key_benefits: string[] }.

The approach is one paragraph (typically 4-8 sentences) that names a specific artefact at a specific path, with a specific shape, wired into a specific lifecycle gate (CI / pre-merge / pre-deploy / ARB review / quarterly review). The reader should be able to picture what to build and where to wire it from the approach alone — not in vendor-specific terms (Bedrock, Vertex AI) but in catalogue-specific concrete terms (path conventions, file shapes, gate conditions). The approach implements what the statement mandates; it does not introduce new architectural ideas not implied by the statement.

The key_benefits are an array of 3-5 short items, each one a single sentence (occasionally two) naming a benefit and explaining the mechanism. Each benefit should map to a failure mode named in the problem field — the benefits add up to 'the failure modes problem named are addressed by this solution'. Avoid generic benefits ('improves quality', 'reduces risk'). Name what specifically becomes possible because the artefact + enforcement is in place.

DIMENSIONS YOUR DRAFT WILL BE SCORED ON

Full rubric in sections/solution/rubric.json. The six dimensions:

1. names_concrete_artefact — Approach names a specific artefact at a specific path with a specific shape.
2. names_enforcement_mechanism — Approach names how the artefact is wired into a lifecycle gate (CI, PR review, ARB, quarterly).
3. derives_from_statement — Approach implements the statement; doesn't introduce new architectural ideas.
4. benefits_map_to_problem — key_benefits collectively address the failure modes named in problem.
5. vendor_neutral_implementation — No specific vendor tooling in approach. Vendor specifics belong in framework_mappings notes.
6. parallel_form_with_siblings — Approach length / vocabulary / shape matches siblings; key_benefits count is 3-5.

All six must score ≥ 2 to pass. Draft with these in mind from the start.

COMMON FAILURE MODES TO AVOID

- Approach describes a goal, not an implementation ('the team should monitor X', 'mechanisms must be in place to ensure Y'). Score low on names_concrete_artefact. Name the artefact and where it lives.
- Approach introduces architectural ideas not in the statement (e.g. statement says 'maintain a harness', approach invents a 'verification chain' concept). Score low on derives_from_statement.
- Benefits don't reference failures from problem — they're generic motherhood ('agents perform better', 'team has more confidence'). Score low on benefits_map_to_problem. Tie each benefit back to a specific failure problem named.
- Vendor-specific tooling in approach ('use the Bedrock evaluation pipeline', 'configure Vertex AI Model Monitoring'). Score low on vendor_neutral_implementation. Vendor specifics go in framework_mappings.
- No enforcement mechanism named ('the harness must be of high quality' — by what gate?). Score low on names_enforcement_mechanism. Name the CI gate, the ARB review point, or the quarterly check.
user_template:
You are authoring the solution field for the principle with these properties:

<principle_id>{{ principle_id }}</principle_id>
<pillar>{{ pillar }}</pillar>
<focus_area>{{ focus_area }}</focus_area>

<aws_anchor>
  <best_practice>{{ aws_bp }}</best_practice>
  <implementation_step>
    <number>{{ aws_step_number }}</number>
    <title>{{ aws_step_title }}</title>
    <verbatim_text>{{ aws_step_verbatim }}</verbatim_text>
  </implementation_step>
</aws_anchor>

<statement>
  <title>{{ statement_title }}</title>
  <description>{{ statement_description }}</description>
</statement>

<problem>
  <description>{{ problem_description }}</description>
  <examples>{{ problem_examples }}</examples>
</problem>

<siblings_under_same_bp>
{{ sibling_solutions }}
</siblings_under_same_bp>

Draft the solution (approach + key_benefits) for this principle. Return the JSON object specified in the output contract.

schema.key_benefits: string[] — 3-5 items, one sentence each (occasionally two)

example_passing_output.approach: Commit the test harness to the workload repository at a known path (convention: `eval/`, with `eval/scenarios/` for labelled test cases and `eval/data/` for supporting input data). Wire the harness into the CI pipeline so it runs on every PR that changes agent code or configuration. Migration to production is blocked when the harness fails.
key_benefits: Foundational dependency for periodic evaluation, drift monitoring, and harness refresh — none of those can run without this principle in place.
Production knowledge survives team changes; the harness is in the repository, not in any individual's head or local notebook.
Structured schema (input + expected decision + expected evidence) lets the evaluation pipeline grade both decisions AND reasoning paths.
Re-labelling work is bounded — entries are first-class repository artefacts with diff tooling for label changes, not opaque blobs.
